title: ABAP AMDP — Database Procedure Design
description: Procedural logic pushed to HANA. AMDP class design patterns, SQLScript vs ABAP boundary decision framework, CDS table function integration, exception handling across ABAP/HANA boundary, debugging, and memory limits.

sections.title: AMDP Class Design Patterns

description.value:
AMDP (ABAP Managed Database Procedures) allow you to write HANA SQLScript procedures directly in ABAP classes, with full lifecycle management (activation, transport, versioning). The key architectural insight is that AMDP methods must be stateless — they cannot access the ABAP application server in any way. All data must come from the database or be passed as parameters. This statelessness is what enables HANA to cache execution plans aggressively across different calls and different users.

Class Design Principles: (1) Keep AMDP methods stateless — no instance attributes, no static data, no ABAP object references. (2) Group related AMDP methods into dedicated AMDP classes (e.g., ZCL_AMDP_SALES, ZCL_AMDP_MATERIAL). (3) Use factory or dependency injection patterns to consume AMDP classes from business logic. (4) Never mix ABAP and SQLScript in the same method — either an AMDP method or a regular ABAP method, never both.

Why Stateless Matters: HANA's plan cache uses the SQL text as the cache key. If your AMDP method uses instance variables or session context that varies per call, the plan cache hit rate drops to zero. Stateless AMDP methods produce the same SQL text for the same input parameters → maximum plan reuse.

Staff+ Insight: Stateless database procedures are the same pattern used in distributed databases (like Apache Spark's UDFs, Google BigQuery's persistent UDFs, or PostgreSQL's immutable functions). The principle is universal: maximize plan cache utilization by making procedures deterministic with respect to their inputs.

CLASS ZCL_AMDP_SALES DEFINITION
  PUBLIC
  CREATE PUBLIC .

  PUBLIC SECTION.
    INTERFACES ZIF_AMDP_SALES.

  PROTECTED SECTION.
  PRIVATE SECTION.
ENDCLASS.

CLASS ZCL_AMDP_SALES IMPLEMENTATION.

  METHOD zif_amdp_sales~get_sales_forecast
    BY DATABASE PROCEDURE FOR HDB
    LANGUAGE SQLSCRIPT
    OPTIONS READ-ONLY
    USING
      I_SalesOrder,
      I_SalesOrderItem,
      I_Product.

    /*
      Staff+ Notes:
      - BY DATABASE PROCEDURE marks this as AMDP (not ABAP)
      - FOR HDB: only HANA database
      - OPTIONS READ-ONLY: procedure does not modify data
      - USING: tables/views this procedure can access
      - All parameters are passed by value (no EXPORTING/CHANGING
        in the traditional ABAP sense — output is via RETURNING
        or OUT parameters)
    */

    lt_sales =
      select so.SalesOrderID,
             so.TotalNetAmount,
             soi.ProductID,
             p.ProductName
        from I_SalesOrder so
        inner join I_SalesOrderItem soi
          on so.SalesOrderID = soi.SalesOrderID
        inner join I_Product p
          on soi.ProductID = p.ProductID
        where so.CreationDate between :p_start_date and :p_end_date;

    /* HANA-native aggregation using SQLScript */
    lt_forecast =
      select ProductID,
             ProductName,
             sum(TotalNetAmount) as TotalSales,
             count(distinct SalesOrderID) as OrderCount
        from :lt_sales
        group by ProductID, ProductName
        order by TotalSales desc;

    /* Output assignment */
    et_forecast = :lt_forecast;

  ENDMETHOD.

ENDCLASS.

sections.codeBlock:
CLASS ZCL_AMDP_SALES DEFINITION PUBLIC CREATE PUBLIC.
  PUBLIC SECTION.
    INTERFACES ZIF_AMDP_SALES.
ENDCLASS.

CLASS ZCL_AMDP_SALES IMPLEMENTATION.
  METHOD zif_amdp_sales~get_sales_forecast
    BY DATABASE PROCEDURE FOR HDB
    LANGUAGE SQLSCRIPT
    OPTIONS READ-ONLY
    USING I_SalesOrder I_SalesOrderItem I_Product.

    lt_sales =
      select so.SalesOrderID, so.TotalNetAmount,
             soi.ProductID, p.ProductName
        from I_SalesOrder so
        inner join I_SalesOrderItem soi
          on so.SalesOrderID = soi.SalesOrderID
        inner join I_Product p
          on soi.ProductID = p.ProductID
        where so.CreationDate between :p_start_date and :p_end_date;

    lt_forecast =
      select ProductID, ProductName,
             sum(TotalNetAmount) as TotalSales,
             count(distinct SalesOrderID) as OrderCount
        from :lt_sales
        group by ProductID, ProductName;

    et_forecast = :lt_forecast;
  ENDMETHOD.
ENDCLASS.

decisionTree.prompt: How should I structure AMDP classes for maintainability?
decisionTree.firstQuestion: "Is this AMDP a stateless, reusable procedure or a one-off data migration script?" Stateless reusable procedures should be in dedicated AMDP classes with interfaces. One-off scripts can be inline AMDP methods.

branches.condition: Reusable procedure called by multiple consumers
branches.path: Dedicated AMDP class with interface. Define INTERFACE ZIF_AMDP_SALES with method declarations. Implement in ZCL_AMDP_SALES. Enables plan cache reuse and unit testing via test doubles.

branches.condition: One-off data migration or ad-hoc analysis
branches.path: Inline AMDP method in the consuming class. Use BY DATABASE PROCEDURE directly in the report or migration class. Simpler, no interface overhead. Can be refactored later.
AMDP class with BY DATABASE FUNCTION method. Must implement the exact signature defined by the CDS Table Function. The @ObjectModel annotations on the CDS side control optimizer behavior.

decisionTree.staffPlusTip: Stateless AMDP methods produce the same SQL text for the same inputs — maximizing HANA plan cache hit rate. Never use instance attributes in AMDP classes.

sections.title: SQLScript vs ABAP — The Boundary Decision

description.value:
The decision to push logic into HANA (via AMDP) or keep it in ABAP is an architectural tradeoff that Staff+ engineers must evaluate carefully. The wrong choice can lead to unmaintainable SQLScript (too much pushed) or performance bottlenecks (too much kept in ABAP). This is the same tradeoff as stored procedures vs application code in any database system.

Push to HANA when: (1) The operation involves large data volumes — filtering, aggregation, sorting millions of rows. Moving data to the application server for processing incurs network transfer cost. (2) The logic is set-based — JOINs, UNIONs, window functions, grouping. SQLScript excels at set operations; ABAP does not (LOOP + nested LOOP is O(n*m)). (3) The logic requires HANA-specific features — full-text search, spatial, predictive, series data. (4) The result feeds another CDS view or analytical query — processing must happen in-database for consumption through the CDS stack.

Keep in ABAP when: (1) The logic requires SAP-specific context — user authorizations (beyond DCL), business partner checks, customizing table reads with complex fallback logic. (2) The logic is control-flow heavy — deeply nested conditionals, complex sequential processing, state machines. SQLScript has limited control flow compared to ABAP. (3) The logic requires external service calls — RFC, HTTP, file system access. AMDP cannot call external systems. (4) The data volume is small (less than ~10,000 rows) — the overhead of the AMDP call context switch may exceed the processing time.

2s ease-in-out infinite}@keyframes pulse{0%,100%{opacity:.6}50%{opacity:1}}</style><defs><marker id="arrow" markerWidth="8" markerHeight="6" refX="8" refY="3" orient="auto"><path d="M0,0 L8,3 L0,6 Z" fill="var(--border-default)"/></marker></defs><rect class="bd-box" x="15" y="15" width="670" height="390"/><text class="bd-lbl" x="350" y="42">AMDP vs ABAP: Decision Framework</text><ellipse class="bd-decision" cx="350" cy="100" rx="100" ry="30"/><text class="bd-lbl" x="350" y="105">What is the logic?</text><line x1="280" y1="130" x2="180" y2="165" class="bd-flow"/><line x1="420" y1="130" x2="520" y2="165" class="bd-flow"/><rect class="bd-abap" x="60" y="165" width="240" height="100"/><text class="bd-lbl" x="180" y="185">Keep in ABAP</text><text class="bd-sub" x="180" y="205">• Small data volumes (<10K rows)</text><text class="bd-sub" x="180" y="220">• External service calls (RFC/HTTP)</text><text class="bd-sub" x="180" y="235">• Complex control flow / state machines</text><text class="bd-sub" x="180" y="250">• SAP context (auth, customizing)</text><rect class="bd-hana" x="400" y="165" width="240" height="100"/><text class="bd-lbl" x="520" y="185">Push to HANA (AMDP)</text><text class="bd-sub" x="520" y="205">• Large data volumes (millions)</text><text class="bd-sub" x="520" y="220">• Set-based operations (JOIN, GROUP)</text><text class="bd-sub" x="520" y="235">• HANA-specific features (search, spatial)</text><text class="bd-sub" x="520" y="250">• CDS table function integration</text><line x1="180" y1="265" x2="180" y2="295" class="bd-flow"/><line x1="520" y1="265" x2="520" y2="295" class="bd-flow"/><rect class="bd-box" x="60" y="295" width="240" height="80"/><text class="bd-lbl" x="180" y="315">Tradeoff: Maintainability</text><text class="bd-sub" x="180" y="335">• ABAP: easier to debug, richer tooling</text><text class="bd-sub" x="180" y="350">• AMDP: harder to test in isolation</text><rect class="bd-box" x="400" y="295" width="240" height="80"/><text class="bd-lbl" x="520" y="315">Tradeoff: Performance</text><text class="bd-sub" x="520" y="335">• HANA: no data transfer, parallel exec</text><text class="bd-sub" x="520" y="350">• ABAP: context switch overhead</text></svg>

* Decision: Push to HANA or keep in ABAP

* CASE 1: Large data volume → AMDP
* --------------------------------------------------
* ABAP pattern (BAD for large data):
*   SELECT * FROM I_SalesOrder INTO TABLE @lt_so.
*   LOOP AT lt_so INTO ls_so.
*     SELECT SUM(TotalNetAmount) FROM I_SalesOrderItem
*       WHERE SalesOrderID = ls_so-SalesOrderID ...
*   ENDLOOP.
*   → N+1 queries, network transfer of millions of rows
*
* AMDP pattern (GOOD):
*   Single SQLScript query with GROUP BY + window function
*   → One database roundtrip, all computation in HANA

* CASE 2: External service call → ABAP
* --------------------------------------------------
* AMDP cannot call RFC/HTTP.
* Keep in ABAP:
*   SELECT data FROM database.
*   CALL FUNCTION 'Z_CRM_SERVICE' DESTINATION 'REMOTE'.
*   Process results in ABAP.

* CASE 3: Set-based calculation → AMDP
* --------------------------------------------------
* Moving average calculation:
* SELECT ProductID, CalendarDay,
*        AVG(Quantity) OVER (
*          PARTITION BY ProductID
*          ORDER BY CalendarDay
*          ROWS BETWEEN 29 PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW
*        ) as MovingAvg30Day
*   FROM I_SalesDaily
*
* → HANA window function, single pass over data
* → Impossible to optimize in ABAP without loading all data

sections.codeBlock:
" Push to HANA (large volume, set-based): AMDP
" SELECT + GROUP BY in single SQLScript → one round-trip
"
" Keep in ABAP (external calls, complex control flow):
" SELECT data. CALL FUNCTION 'Z_CRM_SERVICE'. Process in ABAP.
"
" Decision rule:
" IF data_volume > 10000 rows OR logic is set-based THEN AMDP
" ELSE IF external service OR complex branching THEN ABAP
"
" HANA window function example (AMDP only):
" SELECT ProductID, CalendarDay,
"   AVG(Quantity) OVER (
"     PARTITION BY ProductID
"     ORDER BY CalendarDay
"     ROWS BETWEEN 29 PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW
"   ) as MovingAvg30Day
" FROM I_SalesDaily

quantifiedTradeoff.title: SQLScript vs ABAP — The Boundary Decision Cost

variables.name: Network latency
variables.source: AS ABAP to HANA on same server

amdp_sqlscript.monthlyPenalty: $0 — 1M rows processed entirely in HANA. Zero data transfer cost. HANA column store processes 1M rows in ~1-2 seconds for set-based operations. No ABAP work process consumed during execution.

abap_loop.monthlyPenalty: $180 — 0.5s network transfer + 20s ABAP loop time (1M/50K = 20s at 50K rows/sec) + 1.5s FOR ALL ENTRIES SELECTs. Consumes a DIA work process for 22 seconds — blocking other users.

calculation.breakeven: At 10K rows, AMDP is only 2x faster. At 1M rows, AMDP is 11x faster. The breakeven is ~5K rows where context switch overhead equals processing time savings. Below 5K rows, ABAP is simpler to write and debug.

sections.title: CDS Table Function Integration

description.value:
The integration between AMDP and CDS Table Functions is one of the most powerful patterns in the modern ABAP stack. CDS Table Functions provide the declarative interface (metadata, annotations, type system), while AMDP provides the implementation (procedural SQLScript logic). This separation of concerns allows the CDS framework to understand the function's role in the data model while the AMDP handles the procedural complexity.

The lifecycle is: (1) Define a CDS Table Function with define table function — this creates a database-level function wrapper. (2) Implement the Table Function as an AMDP method using BY DATABASE FUNCTION. (3) The CDS view can now reference the Table Function as a data source with full optimization support (predicate pushdown, projection pruning).

@ObjectModel Annotations for Table Functions: These annotations tell the framework how to handle the Table Function's results: @ObjectModel.resultSetType — #CALCULATION_ENGINE for complex multi-step calculations, #FULL_RESULT for functions that return all rows at once, #SCALAR for functions that return a single value. The annotation @ObjectModel.usageType.sizeCategory tells HANA how much memory to allocate.

Staff+ Insight: The CDS Table Function + AMDP pattern implements the strategy pattern at the database layer. The CDS view is the interface (stable contract), and the AMDP is the strategy (replaceable implementation). This allows you to swap implementations without changing consumers — the ultimate goal of abstraction.

@EndUserText.label: 'Complex Stock Calculation via AMDP'
@ObjectModel.resultSetType: #CALCULATION_ENGINE
@ObjectModel.usageType: {
  serviceQuality: #D,
  sizeCategory: #XL,
  dataClass: #MASTER
}
define table function Z_Stock_Projection
  with parameters
    @Environment.systemField: #SYSTEM_DATE
    P_KeyDate     : dats,
    P_Material    : matnr
  returns
{
  key Material    : matnr;
  key Plant       : werks_d;
      StockQty    : menge_d;
      StockValue  : wrbtr;
      Currency    : waers;
      ProjectedATP: menge_d;
}

"""
AMDP Implementation:
"""
CLASS ZCL_AMDP_STOCK IMPLEMENTATION.

  METHOD zif_amdp_stock~stock_projection
    BY DATABASE FUNCTION FOR HDB
    LANGUAGE SQLSCRIPT
    OPTIONS READ-ONLY
    USING
      I_MaterialStock,
      I_MaterialDocument,
      I_Reservation.

    /*
      Multi-step calculation engine:
      Step 1: Current stock from I_MaterialStock
      Step 2: Add incoming goods from material documents
      Step 3: Subtract reservations (outgoing)
      Step 4: Calculate projected ATP (Available to Promise)
    */

    lt_current =
      select Material, Plant, StockQty, StockValue
        from I_MaterialStock
        where Material = :P_Material;

    lt_incoming =
      select Material, Plant,
             sum(Quantity) as IncomingQty
        from I_MaterialDocument
        where Material = :P_Material
          and PostingDate > :P_KeyDate
          and MovementType in ('101','561')
        group by Material, Plant;

    lt_outgoing =
      select Material, Plant,
             sum(RequiredQuantity) as OutgoingQty
        from I_Reservation
        where Material = :P_Material
          and RequirementDate > :P_KeyDate
        group by Material, Plant;

    /* Combine into projection */
    et_result =
      select c.Material,
             c.Plant,
             c.StockQty,
             c.StockValue,
             c.Currency,
             ( c.StockQty
               + coalesce(i.IncomingQty, 0)
               - coalesce(o.OutgoingQty, 0) ) as ProjectedATP
        from :lt_current c
        left outer join :lt_incoming i
          on c.Material = i.Material
          and c.Plant = i.Plant
        left outer join :lt_outgoing o
          on c.Material = o.Material
          and c.Plant = o.Plant;

  ENDMETHOD.

ENDCLASS.

decisionTree.prompt: Should I implement this as a CDS Table Function or a standalone AMDP?
decisionTree.firstQuestion: "Does the result need to be consumed by other CDS views, OData services, or analytics?" If yes, use a CDS Table Function — it exposes the result as a CDS entity. If the result is only consumed by ABAP code, a standalone AMDP is simpler.

branches.condition: Result consumed by CDS views, OData, or Fiori
branches.path: CDS Table Function. Define the interface via define table function. Implement with BY DATABASE FUNCTION. Add @ObjectModel annotations for optimizer hints. Enables predicate pushdown and projection pruning.

branches.condition: Result consumed only by ABAP report or class
branches.path: Standalone AMDP method. Simpler: no CDS view needed. Call directly via method call. Avoids CDS activation overhead during development.

branches.condition: Complex multi-step calculation with intermediate results
branches.path: CDS Table Function with @ObjectModel.resultSetType: #CALCULATION_ENGINE. This annotation tells HANA to allocate sufficient memory and not to inline the function into outer queries.

decisionTree.staffPlusTip: CDS Table Function + AMDP implements the Strategy pattern at the database layer. The CDS view is the stable interface; the AMDP is the replaceable implementation.

sections.title: Exception Handling in AMDP

description.value:
Exception handling across the ABAP/HANA boundary is one of the trickiest aspects of AMDP. When an AMDP method fails, the exception happens in the HANA SQLScript runtime, not in the ABAP runtime. The two runtimes have fundamentally different error handling models: HANA uses database exceptions (SQL errors, constraint violations, deadlocks), while ABAP uses class-based exceptions (CX_ROOT subclasses). Bridging this gap requires careful design.

Error Propagation: When a HANA runtime error occurs during AMDP execution, the database exception is mapped to an ABAP exception: CX_AMDP_EXECUTION_ERROR (execution failure), CX_AMDP_CONNECTION_ERROR (network/database connection), CX_AMDP_TIMEOUT_ERROR (statement timeout), or CX_AMDP_TRANSACTION_ERROR (transaction conflict). The original HANA error code and message are preserved in the exception object's get_text() method.

Transaction Semantics: AMDP methods execute within the calling ABAP LUW (Logical Unit of Work). If the AMDP fails, the entire LUW is rolled back — this is the same as any database operation failure. However, if the AMDP performs DML (INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE), those changes are also rolled back. The key insight is that AMDP does not support autonomous transactions — you cannot commit or rollback independently within the procedure.

SQLScript Exception Handling: Within SQLScript, you can use EXIT HANDLER (equivalent to SQLScript's TRY/CATCH) to catch specific database errors and convert them to more meaningful error codes. You can also use SIGNAL to raise custom exceptions. This allows you to handle expected error conditions (like division by zero, constraint violations) gracefully rather than letting them bubble up as runtime errors.

CLASS ZCL_AMDP_SALES IMPLEMENTATION.

  METHOD zif_amdp_sales~calculate_rebate
    BY DATABASE PROCEDURE FOR HDB
    LANGUAGE SQLSCRIPT
    OPTIONS READ-ONLY
    USING
      I_SalesOrder
      I_CustomerRebate.

    /*
      SQLScript exception handling:
      EXIT HANDLER catches database errors within the procedure.
    */
    DECLARE EXIT HANDLER FOR SQLEXCEPTION
    BEGIN
      /* Transform generic SQL error into meaningful signal */
      SIGNAL sql_error_code 'Z_REBATE_CALC_FAILED'
        SET MESSAGE_TEXT = 'Rebate calculation failed: ' || ::SQL_ERROR_MESSAGE;
    END;

    lt_rebate =
      select so.CustomerID,
             so.SalesOrganization,
             so.TotalNetAmount,
             cr.RebatePercent,
             so.TotalNetAmount * cr.RebatePercent / 100 as RebateAmount
        from I_SalesOrder so
        inner join I_CustomerRebate cr
          on so.CustomerID = cr.CustomerID
          and so.SalesOrganization = cr.SalesOrganization
        where so.CreationDate between :p_start and :p_end;

    et_result = :lt_rebate;

  ENDMETHOD.

ENDCLASS.

"""
ABAP consumer with proper exception handling:
"""
CLASS ZCL_REBATE_PROCESSOR IMPLEMENTATION.

  METHOD calculate_customer_rebates.
    DATA(lo_amdp) = NEW ZCL_AMDP_SALES( ).

    TRY.
        lo_amdp->zif_amdp_sales~calculate_rebate(
          EXPORTING
            p_start = iv_start_date
            p_end   = iv_end_date
          IMPORTING
            et_result = et_rebates ).

      CATCH cx_amdp_execution_error INTO DATA(lx_amdp).
        " Handle HANA execution failure
        " lx_amdp->get_text( ) contains original HANA error
        APPEND INITIAL LINE TO et_messages ASSIGNING FIELD-SYMBOL(<msg>).
        <msg>-type = 'E'.
        <msg>-message = lx_amdp->get_text( ).

      CATCH cx_amdp_timeout_error INTO DATA(lx_timeout).
        " Handle statement timeout (SLT or heavy processing)
        " Retry with smaller date range?
        RAISE EXCEPTION TYPE zcx_rebate_timeout
          EXPORTING
            textid   = zcx_rebate_timeout=>amdp_timeout
            previous = lx_timeout.

      CATCH cx_amdp_transaction_error INTO DATA(lx_txn).
        " Handle deadlock / transaction conflict
        WAIT UP TO 1 SECONDS.
        RETRY.

      CATCH cx_root INTO DATA(lx_other).
        " Catch-all for unexpected ABAP-side errors
        RAISE EXCEPTION TYPE zcx_rebate_general
          EXPORTING
            previous = lx_other.
    ENDTRY.
  ENDMETHOD.

ENDCLASS.

sections.codeBlock:
" SQLScript exception handling
METHOD calculate_rebate
  BY DATABASE PROCEDURE FOR HDB
  LANGUAGE SQLSCRIPT
  OPTIONS READ-ONLY.

  DECLARE EXIT HANDLER FOR SQLEXCEPTION
  BEGIN
    SIGNAL sql_error_code 'Z_REBATE_CALC_FAILED'
      SET MESSAGE_TEXT =
        'Rebate calc failed: ' || ::SQL_ERROR_MESSAGE;
  END;

  lt_rebate =
    select so.CustomerID, so.SalesOrganization,
           so.TotalNetAmount, cr.RebatePercent,
           so.TotalNetAmount * cr.RebatePercent / 100
             as RebateAmount
      from I_SalesOrder so
      inner join I_CustomerRebate cr
        on so.CustomerID = cr.CustomerID
      where so.CreationDate between :p_start and :p_end;
  et_result = :lt_rebate;
ENDMETHOD.

" ABAP consumer
TRY.
    lo_amdp->calculate_rebate(
      EXPORTING p_start = iv_start p_end = iv_end
      IMPORTING et_result = et_rebates ).
  CATCH cx_amdp_execution_error INTO DATA(lx_amdp).
    " lx_amdp->get_text( ) contains original HANA error
ENDTRY.

adversarialResponse.challenge: "Our AMDP exception handling is useless. When the AMDP fails, we get 'CX_AMDP_EXECUTION_ERROR' with a generic message. We can't tell which step failed."
adversarialResponse.badResponse: "Catch the exception and show the full error text." (doesn't address the lack of granularity)
adversarialResponse.goodResponse: "The generic error happens because HANA exceptions are opaque to the ABAP runtime by default — you only get the top-level error code. The fix is to add structured error handling inside your SQLScript. First, use DECLARE EXIT HANDLER FOR SQLEXCEPTION in your AMDP to catch database errors and SIGNAL a custom error code with a specific MESSAGE_TEXT that identifies the failing step. For example, SIGNAL sql_error_code 'Z_STEP_3_FAILED' SET MESSAGE_TEXT = 'Aggregation failed at step 3: ' || ::SQL_ERROR_MESSAGE. Second, add a debug mode parameter to your AMDP that logs intermediate results to a temporary table when active — this lets you trace the exact step that fails without modifying production code. Third, use SQLScript's MAP statement with default error handling for lookup failures. The pattern: IF <condition> THEN SIGNAL ... ELSE ... END IF. This turns a generic 'execution error' into a pinpoint diagnostic with the specific step, input values, and database error code."
adversarialResponse.pattern: proactive-instrumentation — address the root cause (opaque HANA errors) by adding structured SQLScript error handling with EXIT HANDLER, SIGNAL, and debug mode logging.

sections.title: Debugging AMDP

description.value:
Debugging AMDP procedures requires a different approach than debugging ABAP code. Since the code executes on the HANA database, traditional ABAP debugger tools cannot step through SQLScript line by line within the ABAP runtime. SAP provides several tools specifically for AMDP debugging and analysis.

SQLScript Debugger (SE80 / ADT): The AMDP debugger in ADT (Eclipse) allows you to step through SQLScript code just like ABAP code. You can set breakpoints, inspect variables, watch expressions, and evaluate SQLScript expressions in the debugger console. The debugger is integrated with the HANA database — it sends debug commands over the database protocol. To enable AMDP debugging, you must activate it in your ADT preferences and ensure your database user has debugging privileges.

Transformation Analysis (ST05 / SQL Trace): SQL Trace captures every SQL statement sent to the HANA database. When an AMDP is called, the trace shows the generated HANA procedure call and all SQL statements executed within the procedure. This is critical for understanding: (1) whether the generated SQL is optimal, (2) which indexes are used, (3) whether predicate pushdown is working, (4) the actual execution time of each statement within the procedure.

Plan Visualization (HANA Plan Viz / ST05): The HANA Plan Visualizer shows the execution plan of your AMDP statements, including operator order, join type, parallelism, and memory allocation. This is the Staff+ level tool — you read the plan to identify table scans, missing indexes, suboptimal join orders, and memory spills.

Memory consumed | Rows processed | Parallelism level</text></svg>

"""
  Debugging workflow for AMDP:

  1. Enable AMDP debugging in ADT:
     Window → Preferences → ABAP Development → AMDP Debugging
     Set to 'Enabled' (requires DB debug privileges)

  2. Set breakpoints in SQLScript:
     Double-click left margin in AMDP method body
     or use: BREAK-POINT in ABAP calling method

  3. SQL Trace (ST05):
     /nST05 → Activate Trace → Execute ABAP program
     → Deactivate Trace → Display Trace
     Look for: EXECUTE PROCEDURE statements

  4. HANA Plan Visualization:
     From ST05 trace entry → 'Explain' → opens HANA PlanViz
     Key operators to inspect:
     - TABLE SCAN (full table scan — consider index)
     - JOIN (check join type: HASH/MERGE/NESTED LOOP)
     - AGGREGATION (check GROUP BY pushed down)
     - MEMORY SPILL (data exceeded available memory)
"""

" Example: Debugging suspicious AMDP performance
METHOD analyze_amdp_performance.
  " Activate SQL Trace programmatically
  " (requires authorization S_ADMI_FCD)

  " Execute the AMDP call
  DATA(lo_amdp) = NEW zcl_amdp_sales( ).
  lo_amdp->zif_amdp_sales~get_sales_forecast(
    EXPORTING
      p_start_date = iv_start
      p_end_date   = iv_end
    IMPORTING
      et_forecast  = DATA(lt_result) ).

  " Check execution via STAD (transaction) or ST05
  " Look for multiple calls — the AMDP should be called once
  " If you see N calls for N input records, you have N+1 problem

  " Alternatively: check DB time vs ABAP time
  " If ABAP time > 70% of total, AMDP overhead is too high
  " Consider merging multiple small AMDP calls into one
ENDMETHOD.

sections.codeBlock:
" Debugging workflow:
" 1. ADT: Window → Preferences → AMDP Debugging → Enabled
" 2. Set breakpoints in SQLScript (double-click left margin)
" 3. ST05 SQL Trace: /nST05 → Activate → Execute → Display
"    Look for EXECUTE PROCEDURE statements
" 4. HANA PlanViz: From ST05 trace → 'Explain'
"    Key operators: TABLE SCAN, JOIN type, AGGREGATION, MEMORY SPILL

METHOD analyze_amdp_performance.
  DATA(lo_amdp) = NEW zcl_amdp_sales( ).
  lo_amdp->zif_amdp_sales~get_sales_forecast(
    EXPORTING p_start_date = iv_start p_end_date = iv_end
    IMPORTING et_forecast = DATA(lt_result) ).
  " Check ST05: should be 1 procedure call, not N
  " If ABAP time > 70% of total, AMDP overhead too high
ENDMETHOD.

quantifiedTradeoff.title: AMDP Debugging Tools — Time Investment vs Diagnostic Value

st05_trace.monthlyPenalty: $333 — 5 issues × (2min setup + 3min trace read) × $100/h = $42. Also captures SQL text, duration, and row counts. Best first step for any AMDP issue.

adp_debugger.monthlyPenalty: $2,000 — 5 issues × (5min setup + 25min stepping) × $100/h = $250. Step-by-step debugging is powerful but time-consuming. Use only when the trace shows unexpected intermediate results.

plan_viz.monthlyPenalty: $3,750 — 5 issues × (10min setup + 35min analysis) × $100/h = $375. Use for performance issues only (memory spills, suboptimal join order, missing indexes). Not for logic bugs.

calculation.breakeven: ST05 trace is the most cost-effective first step for 80% of issues. Use PlanViz only when trace shows high duration but low row counts (index/join problem). Use ADT debugger only for logic bugs in complex stored procedures.

sections.title: Memory Limits & Parallel Execution

description.value:
AMDP execution is subject to HANA's memory governance framework. Understanding these limits is critical for building robust AMDP procedures that don't crash under heavy load. At FAANG-scale data volumes (millions to billions of rows), memory management is often the limiting factor, not CPU.

HANA Memory Governor: Each SQLScript procedure runs within a HANA statement-level memory limit, governed by the parameter statement_memory_limit (default: 2GB). If the procedure exceeds this limit, HANA aborts the statement with error SQL_ERROR_MEMORY_LIMIT_EXCEEDED (SQL code 257). You can override this limit per-session, but this requires DBA privileges and careful consideration of overall HANA memory pressure.

Parallel Execution: HANA automatically parallelizes SQLScript execution when the database detects independent sub-operations. This is controlled by the parallel_execution_enabled parameter and the number of available CPU cores. AMDP procedures benefit from this parallelism naturally — no explicit threading is required. However, you can influence parallelism through: (1) Table partitioning — partitioned tables enable partition-level parallelism. (2) UNION ALL branches — each branch can execute in parallel. (3) Multiple SELECT statements — HANA can execute independent SELECTs in parallel.

Buffer Pool & Memory Spilling: If an AMDP operation (like sorting or hash join) exceeds the allocated buffer pool, HANA spills to disk. This slows performance dramatically (disk I/O is 3-5 orders of magnitude slower than memory). The SQL_TRACING tool reveals spilling events. To avoid spilling: ensure adequate sort memory (max_sort_memory), use LIMIT/OFFSET for pagination, and avoid aggregation of unconstrained result sets.

"""
  Memory-Safe AMDP Design Patterns
"""

CLASS ZCL_AMDP_LARGE_DATA IMPLEMENTATION.

  METHOD zif_amdp_large~process_millions
    BY DATABASE PROCEDURE FOR HDB
    LANGUAGE SQLSCRIPT
    OPTIONS READ-ONLY
    USING
      I_LargeTable
      I_ReferenceTable.

    /*
      Anti-pattern: Load everything into memory
      BAD:
        lt_all = select * from I_LargeTable;
        → memory spill for large tables

      Good pattern: Use pagination or streaming
    */

    /* Option 1: Process in chunks using WHERE range */
    lt_chunk =
      select * from I_LargeTable
        where ID between :p_start_id and :p_end_id;

    /* Option 2: Use aggregate pushdown */
    lt_summary =
      select Category,
             count(*) as RecordCount,
             sum(Amount) as TotalAmount
        from I_LargeTable
        where ProcessedDate between :p_start and :p_end
        group by Category;
    /* → HANA computes aggregation, only returns summary rows */

    /* Option 3: Streaming via cursor (inside SQLScript) */
    FOR cur AS cur_rec CURSOR FOR
      select ID, Amount from I_LargeTable
        where Category = :p_category
        order by ID
    DO
      /* Process one row at a time */
      IF :cur_rec.Amount > 1000 THEN
        lt_filtered = select * from :lt_filtered
          union all select :cur_rec.ID, :cur_rec.Amount
                    from dummy;
      END IF;
    END FOR;

    et_result = :lt_filtered;

  ENDMETHOD.

ENDCLASS.

"""
  Monitoring Memory in Production:
  - Transaction DBACOCKPIT → HANA → Memory Configuration
  - SQL Monitoring: /HDB/SQL_MONITOR
  - Check: statement_memory_limit, max_sort_memory
  - Warning signs in ST05 trace:
    * 'Memory spill' in operator details
    * 'Sort' or 'Aggregate' consuming > 500MB
    * Parallelism level = 1 (should be >1 for large tables)
"""

sections.codeBlock:
" Memory-safe AMDP patterns
" Anti-pattern: select * from I_LargeTable; — memory spill
"
" Pattern 1: Process in chunks (WHERE range)
lt_chunk = select * from I_LargeTable
  where ID between :p_start_id and :p_end_id;

" Pattern 2: Aggregate pushdown (summary only)
lt_summary = select Category, count(*) as RecordCount,
    sum(Amount) as TotalAmount
  from I_LargeTable
  where ProcessedDate between :p_start and :p_end
  group by Category;

" Pattern 3: Streaming via cursor
FOR cur AS cur_rec CURSOR FOR
  select ID, Amount from I_LargeTable
    where Category = :p_category
DO
  IF :cur_rec.Amount > 1000 THEN
    lt_filtered = select * from :lt_filtered
      union all select :cur_rec.ID, :cur_rec.Amount from dummy;
  END IF;
END FOR;
et_result = :lt_filtered;

" Monitor: DBACOCKPIT → HANA → Memory Configuration
" Check: statement_memory_limit (default 2GB)
" Warning signs: 'Memory spill' in ST05 trace operator details

quantifiedTradeoff.title: Memory vs Performance Tradeoff in Large AMDP Operations

in_memory.monthlyPenalty: $0 — 50M × 200B = 10GB raw data + ~5GB intermediate = 15GB. Requires increasing statement_memory_limit from 2GB to 16GB. Parallel execution across 8 CPU cores.

chunked_disk.monthlyPenalty: $1,200 — Processing in 2GB chunks: 8 passes × ~55s each. Disk I/O for spilling intermediate results is 15x slower than in-memory. No DBA intervention needed.

calculation.breakeven: At <5GB total data, in-memory is always better. At 15GB+, the DBA cost of increasing statement_memory_limit ($500 one-time tuning) pays for itself after 3 months of monthly runs. At 50GB+, switch to chunked processing regardless of memory.

adversarialResponse.challenge: "Our AMDP crashes every month-end with 'SQL_ERROR_MEMORY_LIMIT_EXCEEDED' when processing the financial close."
adversarialResponse.badResponse: "Ask the DBA to increase statement_memory_limit." (pushes responsibility, no architectural fix)
adversarialResponse.goodResponse: "The memory limit error (SQL code 257) means your AMDP exceeds the 2GB default statement_memory_limit. There are three approaches, each with different tradeoffs: (1) Short-term fix: request DBA to increase statement_memory_limit to 8GB for the specific RFC user that runs this AMDP. Use ALTER USER SET STATEMENT MEMORY LIMIT. Risk: if another large query runs concurrently, HANA may OOM. (2) Architectural fix: rewrite the AMDP to process data in chunks. Add a WHILE loop in SQLScript that processes date ranges or material ranges, accumulating results with UNION ALL. This keeps each chunk under 2GB. The tradeoff: slightly slower (chunking overhead), but doesn't require DBA changes and works regardless of data growth. (3) Strategic fix: push aggregation to the database level. Instead of loading 50M detail rows into memory, use HANA's aggregate pushdown — GROUP BY at the table level, returning only summary rows. The SELECT only returns the aggregated result (~1K rows), avoiding the memory issue entirely. I recommend approach 2 for immediate relief and approach 3 for the permanent solution."
adversarialResponse.pattern: tiered-remediation — provide immediate tactical fix (DBA tuning), medium-term architectural fix (chunking), and long-term strategic fix (aggregate pushdown). Quantify the tradeoff of each approach.

details: Staff+ Insight: AMDP represents database pushdown — a key distributed systems concept. FAANG interviewers will recognize this as "moving computation to data" (like MapReduce, Spark, or stored procedures in any DB). Frame AMDP discussions in this context — data locality, reducing network roundtrips, parallel query execution.
